hq: 1267 Page St, C, San Francisco, CA 94107

system_description.overview: CentralAxis is a Data Center Infrastructure Management (DCIM) platform that provides monitoring, asset tracking, and compliance automation for data centers. It collects telemetry from on-premises data center devices via SNMP/IPMI polling and surfaces it through a web-based UI hosted on Vercel.

products.CentralAxis Platform: Web-based DCIM platform for monitoring, asset tracking, power/cooling management, and compliance automation across data center infrastructure

infrastructure.physical_presence: 1267 Page St, C, San Francisco, CA 94107

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Segmented VPCs with ACLs; separate network zones for Vercel edge, Supabase, and AWS EC2 instances
network_architecture.firewalls: Vercel built-in firewall; VPC security groups and ACLs on AWS
network_architecture.vpn: VPN bastion host for administrative access to production
network_architecture.tls: TLS enforced for all data in transit
network_architecture.topology: End User Browser → Vercel (Next.js app, logging, firewall, observability) → Supabase (auth, RLS, PostgreSQL org data) + AWS EC2 (InfluxDB time-series); On-premises Utility Node (Node.js CLI, SNMP polling) ← Data Center Devices (SNMP, IPMI)

application_architecture.pattern: Hybrid (Vercel-hosted Next.js frontend + separate backend services on Supabase and AWS EC2)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development, staging, and production environments

authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all production system access
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Minimum length and complexity enforced; unique user IDs required

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider KMS (AWS KMS / Supabase encryption)

ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Branch protection enforced; pull requests required for merges to main
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automated patch management; regular OS and dependency updates

monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured for system thresholds

third_party_services.purpose: Application hosting, CI/CD, edge network, logging, observability, firewall
PostgreSQL database, authentication, row-level security, automated backups
EC2 instances for InfluxDB time-series database, VPC networking, GuardDuty IDS
Time-series data storage for telemetry from data center devices

security_tools.antivirus: Installed on all endpoints; continuous scanning with weekly definition updates

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access controls managed by AWS and Vercel as subservice organizations
A1.2 — Environmental controls managed by building management and cloud providers
notable_controls: Row-level security enforced at Supabase database layer for tenant data isolation
SNMP/IPMI polling from on-premises utility node introduces a hybrid cloud/on-premises boundary
Vercel firewall provides WAF capability at the edge
GuardDuty on AWS EC2 instances for anomaly/intrusion detection

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-AZ AWS for InfluxDB with Vercel edge hosting is solid for a small startup, but no IaC mentioned, no multi-region failover, and heavy reliance on Vercel managed infrastructure limits score.

application_architecture.rationale: Next.js monolith on Vercel with separate Supabase and EC2 backends is functional but not clearly microservices. Branch protection and code review are enforced. No typed language or test suite mentioned.

data_layer.rationale: Two database types (PostgreSQL + InfluxDB) appropriate for use case, daily backups, multi-AZ, per-tenant RLS segregation. No caching layer or data warehouse mentioned.

security_posture.rationale: WAF (Vercel), IDS (GuardDuty), monthly vuln scanning, annual pen test, endpoint AV, MFA enforced, quarterly access reviews. Missing SIEM and cybersecurity insurance disclosure.

devops_maturity.rationale: GitHub + Vercel CI/CD with branch protection and code review is solid. No IaC or container orchestration beyond Vercel managed containers. Separate environments present.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: Policy documented, annually reviewed and tested, multi-AZ, daily backups. No RTO/RPO disclosed. No multi-region failover.

vendor_diversity.rationale: 5 named vendors (Vercel, Supabase, AWS, GitHub, InfluxDB) but Vercel is a single critical dependency for hosting and CI/CD. Heavy concentration risk if Vercel has an outage.

overall.rationale: Competent startup security posture with appropriate controls for a SOC 2 Type 1. Hybrid cloud/on-premises architecture is well-suited to the DCIM use case. Key risks: no IaC, no multi-region, RTO/RPO not disclosed, heavy Vercel dependency.

red_flags: No IaC tooling mentioned — infrastructure changes appear to be manual
RTO and RPO not disclosed despite BCDR policy existing
Hybrid on-premises component (Utility Node with SNMP polling) creates an attack surface not fully in scope of SOC 2 carve-out
yellow_flags: Heavy dependency on Vercel for hosting, CI/CD, WAF, and observability — single vendor concentration risk
No caching layer mentioned — potential performance/scalability concern at scale
No data warehouse or analytics infrastructure mentioned
No SIEM mentioned — log aggregation only via Vercel logging
Trust criteria limited to Security only (Availability, Processing Integrity, Privacy excluded)
green_flags: Two purpose-specific databases (PostgreSQL for organizational data, InfluxDB for time-series telemetry) — good architectural separation
Row-level security at database layer provides strong per-tenant data isolation
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning exceeds typical startup cadence
MFA enforced for all production access
Quarterly access reviews with 1 business day revocation SLA
Zero exceptions in SOC 2 Type 1 report
key_observations: The hybrid cloud/on-premises architecture (Vercel + Supabase + AWS EC2 + on-premises Utility Node) is genuinely appropriate for a DCIM product that must interface with physical data center hardware — this is not just laziness but architectural necessity
Vercel serves as both the CDN/WAF and the CI/CD pipeline — this is an unusual but increasingly common pattern for early-stage startups; it simplifies operations but creates vendor concentration
InfluxDB running on AWS EC2 (rather than a managed time-series service) suggests the team made a deliberate choice, possibly for cost or feature reasons
Supabase RLS for tenant isolation is a strong pattern that goes beyond many peers at this stage
The on-premises Utility Node using Node.js CLI with SNMP polling represents a unique technical component not covered by cloud security controls — its security posture merits additional diligence
